instruction: Design a codebase onboarding experience that gets new developers productive within their first week.
output:
<think>
Onboarding should be layered by urgency. Day 1: environment setup must be ONE command (make setup or docker compose up). Run the app locally. Make a trivial change and see it. Day 2-3: architecture tour — a short document (under 5 pages) explaining the major components, data flow, and where to find things. Paired with a mentor who answers questions in real-time. Day 3-5: first real PR on a well-scoped starter issue. The mentor reviews with extra context about conventions. Essential artifacts: one-command setup, architecture diagram, glossary of domain terms (every project has jargon), annotated examples of good PRs showing conventions. Anti-patterns: 50-page wiki that is never read, expecting new developers to figure out setup from scattered READMEs, no designated mentor, giving a large complex task as first assignment.
</think>

instruction: Reason about what makes a codebase approachable vs intimidating for new developers.
output:
<think>
Approachable codebase characteristics: consistent patterns (once you learn one service, you can navigate all of them), good error messages (tell you what went wrong AND how to fix it), fast feedback loops (tests run in seconds, not minutes), clear directory structure that matches mental model, README with quick start that actually works. Intimidating codebase characteristics: clever abstractions that require deep context to understand, implicit conventions documented only in tribal knowledge, slow builds that break flow, circular dependencies that make it hard to understand any piece in isolation, no tests (so you cannot safely change anything), over-abstraction (five layers of indirection for a simple operation). Measurement: time from git clone to first successful test run. If this is more than 30 minutes, the onboarding experience needs work.
</think>


instruction: Design a contribution guide that lowers the barrier for open source first-time contributors.
output:
<think>
Good contribution guide minimizes friction at every step. Structure: (1) Prerequisites — exact versions, one-command install. (2) Development setup — clone, install deps, verify with make test. (3) Making changes — code style enforced by linter (not prose rules), type checking, test requirements. (4) Submitting — PR template with checklist, expected review timeline. (5) Good first issues — labeled issues that are small, well-scoped, and have implementation hints. Anti-patterns: requiring Contributor License Agreement before first PR (creates legal friction), complex branch naming conventions, mandatory interactive rebase (confusing for Git beginners), review comments that are critical without being constructive. Best practice: comment on the first PR with encouragement, be explicit about what is good and what needs changing, offer to pair on complex feedback.
</think>


instruction: Plan an architecture tour document that explains the system without overwhelming new team members.
output:
<think>
Architecture tour structure: start with the user journey, not the code structure. "When a user searches for a product, here is what happens: (1) Browser sends request to API gateway [/api directory]. (2) Gateway authenticates and routes to Search service [/services/search]. (3) Search service queries Elasticsearch [/services/search/repository.py]. (4) Results are enriched with pricing from Price service [/services/pricing]. (5) Response is formatted and returned." This gives the new developer a mental model they can hang code exploration on. Include: one architecture diagram (not more — multiple diagrams are never kept in sync), the data flow for the two or three most important user journeys, key abstractions and where they live, common patterns used across the codebase, where to look for different types of code (tests here, configs there, migrations over here). Keep under 5 pages. Link to deeper docs for each component, but the tour itself must be concise.
</think>
